guest: Suleiman Ali, founder of TinyCo and Native, investor via Ali Capital

business_ideas.idea: Superlatives, a Facebook app where friends give each other funny superlative titles
Twitter for colleges, a local social network for campus events
TinyCo mobile games licensing major media brands (Family Guy, Harry Potter, Marvel)
Native, an all-natural deodorant brand tested by sniffing armpits at home
A new stealth-mode company already generating significant revenue

predictions.text: The next 20 years will be about hacking human biology and genetics, not just software.
Engineering will become a commodity in Silicon Valley; well-rounded non-technical founders will rise.

dynamics: Single host (Sam Parr, in this earliest 'How I Got Rich'-style format) drives with structured biographical questions while the guest narrates in long uninterrupted arcs; host occasionally interjects to highlight a lesson or ask him to double-click. Energy is reflective and admiring rather than riffing; no idea-brainstorm segment or ad breaks noted, reflecting the show's pre-cohost, single-guest-narrative original format.
frameworks: Look reality in the face, not away from it: proactively hunt for evidence your business is failing rather than confirming success
Pre-mortem your business: list all reasons it could fail before writing a line of code and try to disprove them
learnings: Big companies reward incremental promotion but rarely give someone true authorship over a product, which is why some ambitious employees leave even when treated well.
People tend to overestimate the career risk of quitting a stable job, especially in a tight labor market for skilled workers.
Vanity metrics (total-ever users) can mask the fact you're not building lasting engagement or repeat usage.
Sheer persistence in a high-stakes negotiation (calling a partner every day, enlisting board members' networks) can unlock deals that seem otherwise unreachable.
Founders often lose sight of reality because everyone around them (investors, press, employees, friends) rewards them for projecting optimism.
Founder's own emotional resolve can function as a company's psychological life-support system during near-death periods.
Betting personal money back into a failing company can be as much an emotional decision as a financial one.

banger_quotes.quote: I felt like I was in a prison of my own making by being at Microsoft.
You want to look reality in the face or reality is in the room and you're looking away from it.
It's really easy to get high on your own supply.
I got to move on with the rest of my life, this chapter is over.

numbers_dropped: Sold first company (Facebook apps) for $2M after 9 months
Naval Ravikant offered a $200K wire on the spot for equity
TinyCo grew to $20M revenue year one, $40M year two
Paid Fox $10M for the Family Guy license, funded via a Silicon Valley Bank loan
Family Guy game generated 150-200M+ in revenue over time
TinyCo sold while doing ~$85M revenue and $15M EBITDA
Turned a $100K angel investment in MoPub into $1M after Twitter's acquisition
Native deodorant sold to Procter & Gamble in a nine-figure deal
notable: This is episode 1 in a single-host 'How I Got Rich'-style format predating the Sam/Shaan co-host dynamic that defines later MFM episodes.
